definition: The range of effects that oak contact — whether from barrel fermentation, barrel ageing, or alternative oak products (staves, chips, inner staves) — has on a wine's aroma, flavour, texture, and structure. Oak contributes two categories of effect: (1) direct flavour compounds extracted from the wood (vanilla from vanillin, coconut from lactones, spice from eugenol, smoke/toast from pyrazines and furans); and (2) the controlled micro-oxygenation effect of oxygen permeation through barrel staves, which softens tannins and develops complexity.
distinction_insights: New oak and old oak behave differently: new oak contributes high levels of extractable compounds (vanillin, tannin); old oak has been depleted of extractable compounds and functions primarily as a micro-oxygenation vessel.

Toast level (light, medium, heavy) affects which compounds are extracted: light toast preserves more vanillin and lactones; heavy toast produces more toasted/smoky pyrazines and reduces extractable tannin.

Oak origin matters: French oak (Quercus petraea / robur) has finer grain, more subtle flavour integration, and contributes silkier tannins; American oak (Quercus alba) has wider grain, releases more vanillin and lactones (coconut, vanilla) more quickly.

Micro-oxygenation through barrel staves promotes tannin polymerisation, which reduces perceived astringency — this is distinct from the flavour contribution and explains why barrel-aged reds feel softer even from new oak.
Alternative oak products (chips, staves) can replicate flavour extraction but cannot replicate micro-oxygenation — they produce different results from barrel ageing.
